items.title: Jury unanimously dismisses Elon Musk's lawsuit against OpenAI
items.standfirst: California jury dismissed all charges, finding that Musk missed the three-year statute of limitations to file suit.
items.content: California jury dismissed all charges, finding that Musk missed the three-year statute of limitations to file suit.

items.title: Elon Musk just lost another lawsuit. Will he keep fighting?
items.standfirst: Musk's loss against OpenAI is the latest in a string of courtroom defeats.
items.content: Musk's loss against OpenAI is the latest in a string of courtroom defeats.

items.title: Musk loses OpenAI court battle after jury finds he waited too long to sue
items.standfirst: Jurors spent weeks hearing about Musk's claim that Altman had "stolen a charity."
items.content: Jurors spent weeks hearing about Musk's claim that Altman had "stolen a charity."

items.title: What to know after jury tosses Elon Musk lawsuit against OpenAI, Sam Altman
items.standfirst: A jury on Monday unanimously dismissed Elon Musk's lawsuit against OpenAI and Sam Altman due to the expired statute of limitations. CBS News' Jo Ling Kent and Jessica Levinson have more.
items.content: A jury on Monday unanimously dismissed Elon Musk's lawsuit against OpenAI and Sam Altman due to the expired statute of limitations. CBS News' Jo Ling Kent and Jessica Levinson have more.

items.title: Jury Says Elon Musk Sued Sam Altman Too Late Over OpenAI
items.standfirst: A federal jury rejects Elon Musk's claims about OpenAI under Sam Altman and says he waited too long to sue. Musk took Altman to trial saying he went against OpenAI's mission by turning it into a for-profit business. Matthew Schettenhelm of Bloomberg Intelligence has been following the case and has more. (Source: Bloomberg)
items.content: A federal jury rejects Elon Musk's claims about OpenAI under Sam Altman and says he waited too long to sue. Musk took Altman to trial saying he went against OpenAI's mission by turning it into a for-profit business. Matthew Schettenhelm of Bloomberg Intelligence has been following the case and has more. (Source: Bloomberg)

items.title: Jury dismisses all claims in Elon Musk's lawsuit against OpenAI CEO Sam Altman
items.standfirst: Musk had sought to oust Altman from his leadership position over claims that he and others breached their duty to OpenAI's original nonprofit mission and unjustly enriched themselves.
items.content: Musk had sought to oust Altman from his leadership position over claims that he and others breached their duty to OpenAI's original nonprofit mission and unjustly enriched themselves.(Image credit: Godofredo A. Vásquez)

items.content: Elon Musk has lost his lawsuit against OpenAI and its chief executive Sam Altman.

items.title: Elon Musk Loses Case Against Sam Altman Over OpenAI’s Future
items.standfirst: A jury rejected Elon Musk’s claims that OpenAI under Sam Altman’s leadership betrayed its mission to benefit the public by morphing into a for-profit business, finding that he waited too long to sue the company.
items.content: A jury rejected Elon Musk’s claims that OpenAI under Sam Altman’s leadership betrayed its mission to benefit the public by morphing into a for-profit business, finding that he waited too long to sue the company.

items.title: Elon Musk Loses Landmark Lawsuit Against OpenAI
items.standfirst: The nine-member panel took only two hours to return a verdict in favor of OpenAI on Monday, which the judge quickly adopted as her own final decision.
items.content: The nine-member panel took only two hours to return a verdict in favor of OpenAI on Monday, which the judge quickly adopted as her own final decision.

items.title: Jury to start deliberations in Elon Musk lawsuit against OpenAI, Sam Altman
items.standfirst: A nine-person jury will begin deliberations Monday in Elon Musk's $150 billion civil lawsuit against OpenAI CEO Sam Altman. Rachel Metz, artificial intelligence reporter for Bloomberg, joins with more.
items.content: A nine-person jury will begin deliberations Monday in Elon Musk's $150 billion civil lawsuit against OpenAI CEO Sam Altman. Rachel Metz, artificial intelligence reporter for Bloomberg, joins with more.

clusters.title: At least 100 dead in Ebola outbreak in DR Congo, official says

Reported by BBC World, France24, CBS News and others

items.standfirst: The World Health Organization (WHO) has declared the outbreak an international emergency.

items.title: Ebola: DR Congo outbreak a global health emergency, says WHO
items.standfirst: An outbreak of a rare strain of the Ebola virus in DR Congo was declared “a public health emergency of international concern” by the World Health Organization on Sunday. The outbreak in the eastern Ituri province has already caused at least 91 deaths, with around 350 suspected cases.
items.content: An outbreak of a rare strain of the Ebola virus in DR Congo was declared “a public health emergency of international concern” by the World Health Organization on Sunday. The outbreak in the eastern Ituri province has already caused at least 91 deaths, with around 350 suspected cases.

clusters.title: French cinema faces reckoning as media mogul Bolloré blacklists stars for daring to challenge him
clusters.neutral_headline: 'A naive view of regulation of politics in media' in France, expert says
bullet_summary: The head of France's biggest film producer, Canal+, said that the group would no longer work with 600 industry professionals who signed a petition against right-wing billionaire owner Vincent Bolloré
Reported by France24, Guardian Business

items.title: French cinema faces reckoning as media mogul Bolloré blacklists stars for daring to challenge him
items.standfirst: France's film industry is reeling after its biggest production company – Canal+, owned by right-wing billionaire Vincent Bolloré – announced it would no longer work with some 600 professionals who signed a petition opposing Bolloré and the "grip of the far right" on cinema. The fallout is highlighting some uncomfortable truths about the industry's reliance on one company.
items.content: France's film industry is reeling after its biggest production company – Canal+, owned by right-wing billionaire Vincent Bolloré – announced it would no longer work with some 600 professionals who signed a petition opposing Bolloré and the "grip of the far right" on cinema. The fallout is highlighting some uncomfortable truths about the industry's reliance on one company.

items.title: 'A naive view of regulation of politics in media' in France, expert says
items.standfirst: The head of France's biggest film producer, Canal+, said Sunday that the group would no longer work with 600 industry professionals who signed a petition against right-wing billionaire owner Vincent Bolloré. Speaking with FRANCE 24's Mark Owen, Renaud Foucart, Senior Lecturer in Economics at Lancaster University, highlights the risk of media concentration, saying that "France has always perhaps had a naive view of regulation of politics in media".
items.content: The head of France's biggest film producer, Canal+, said Sunday that the group would no longer work with 600 industry professionals who signed a petition against right-wing billionaire owner Vincent Bolloré. Speaking with FRANCE 24's Mark Owen, Renaud Foucart, Senior Lecturer in Economics at Lancaster University, highlights the risk of media concentration, saying that "France has always perhaps had a naive view of regulation of politics in media".

items.title: Biggest French cinema producer's blacklist threat could recall Hollywood 'McCarthyism'
items.standfirst: It's an earthquake rocking the world of French cinema. The head of France's biggest film producer, Canal+, said that the group would no longer work with 600 industry professionals who signed a petition against right-wing billionaire owner Vincent Bolloré. The petition called people to mobilise against "the growing grip of the far right" on the film industry under the influence of Bolloré – an accusation Saada has called "an injustice toward the Canal teams". FRANCE 24's Olivia Salazar-Winspear tells us more about what happened, and the context in which the upheaval has happened.
items.content: It's an earthquake rocking the world of French cinema. The head of France's biggest film producer, Canal+, said that the group would no longer work with 600 industry professionals who signed a petition against right-wing billionaire owner Vincent Bolloré. The petition called people to mobilise against "the growing grip of the far right" on the film industry under the influence of Bolloré – an accusation Saada has called "an injustice toward the Canal teams". FRANCE 24's Olivia Salazar-Winspear tells us more about what happened, and the context in which the upheaval has happened.

items.title: France’s top film producer says it will blacklist figures who petitioned against rightwing billionaire
items.standfirst: Canal+ head says he will not work with hundreds of actors and directors who signed protest against Vincent Bolloré’s political swayThe head of France’s biggest film producer, Canal+, has said the group will no longer work with hundreds of cinema figures who signed a petition voicing concern over the growing influence of the rightwing billionaire owner Vincent Bolloré.The open letter, published earlier this week to coincide with the opening of the Cannes film festival, was signed by more than 600 figures, including the actor-director Juliette Binoche, the director and photographer Raymond Depardon, the French-Iranian film-maker Sepideh Farsi and the director Arthur Harari, who co-wrote the Oscar-winning Anatomy of a Fall and is premiering his film The Unknown in the main competition in Cannes. Continue reading...
items.content: Canal+ head says he will not work with hundreds of actors and directors who signed protest against Vincent Bolloré’s political swayThe head of France’s biggest film producer, Canal+, has said the group will no longer work with hundreds of cinema figures who signed a petition voicing concern over the growing influence of the rightwing billionaire owner Vincent Bolloré.The open letter, published earlier this week to coincide with the opening of the Cannes film festival, was signed by more than 600 figures, including the actor-director Juliette Binoche, the director and photographer Raymond Depardon, the French-Iranian film-maker Sepideh Farsi and the director Arthur Harari, who co-wrote the Oscar-winning Anatomy of a Fall and is premiering his film The Unknown in the main competition in Cannes. Continue reading...

items.title: Angela Merkel says Europe ‘not making sufficient use of diplomatic potential’ to engage with Russia – as it happened
items.standfirst: The former German chancellor added that diplomacy alongside military deterrence is crucial when dealing with Russia.Elsewhere, we are getting a line from Rome, with the Italian foreign ministry confirming that rescuers have located the bodies of four Italian divers believed to be deep inside an underwater cave in a Maldive atoll, AP reported.Five Italian divers are believed to have died while exploring a cave at a depth of about 50 meters in Vaavu Atoll on Thursday, according to Italy’s foreign ministry, way below the recreational diving limit of 30 meters. Continue reading...
